title: Page 1
text:
Self-Balancing Robot with Ball Balancing
PID Auto-Tuning via Quantum Computing & Digital Twin
Final Year Project — Complete Pipeline & Recommendations
PHASE 1 — Hardware Design & Assembly
1.1 Mechanical Structure
Define the robot's physical form — a two-wheeled inverted pendulum base with a flat platform on top to hold
the ball. Choose materials such as aluminium frame or 3D-printed chassis. Mount motors, sensors, and the
compute unit, and pre-calculate the centre of mass to assist the control system.
1.2 Actuators
Use DC gear motors or BLDC motors with encoders for wheel control. Motor drivers such as L298N (DC) or
ODrive (BLDC) interface with the main controller.
1.3 Sensors
• IMU (MPU-6050 or MPU-9250) — measures tilt angle and angular velocity for robot balancing.
• Camera, resistive touch panel, or capacitive sensor array — detects ball position on the platform.
• Wheel encoders — measures wheel speed and position for closed-loop control.
1.4 Compute Unit
Raspberry Pi 4 or Jetson Nano as the main controller (SBC). An STM32 or Arduino microcontroller handles
low-level real-time PID loops, while the SBC manages higher-level logic and the quantum interface.
PHASE 2 — Sensor Fusion & State Estimation
2.1 IMU Processing
Apply a Complementary Filter or Kalman Filter to fuse accelerometer and gyroscope data from the IMU to
obtain a clean, accurate tilt angle in real time.
2.2 Ball Position Detection
If using a camera — apply OpenCV-based blob detection or colour tracking to get the (x, y) position of the ball
on the platform. If using a resistive touch panel — directly read analogue (x, y) coordinates.
2.3 Full System State Vector
Define the complete state as: [robot tilt angle, tilt angular velocity, wheel position, wheel velocity, ball
x-position, ball x-velocity, ball y-position, ball y-velocity]. This 8-dimensional state drives both control loops.
PHASE 3 — PID Control System Design
3.1 Cascaded PID Architecture

title: Page 2
text:
The system requires two coupled control loops:
• Inner Loop — Robot Balancing PID: Takes tilt angle error as input and outputs a motor drive signal to
keep the robot upright.
• Outer Loop — Ball Balancing PID: Takes ball position error (deviation from platform centre) as input and
outputs a tilt angle setpoint fed as reference to the inner loop. Tilting the robot slightly nudges the ball
back to centre.
3.2 Decoupling Strategy
The two loops are inherently coupled — tilting the robot to balance the ball disturbs its own balance. Design
the cascade so the inner loop runs significantly faster than the outer loop to maintain stability.
3.3 Initial Gain Estimation
Use classical methods (Ziegler-Nichols or manual tuning) to obtain baseline Kp, Ki, Kd values before
quantum auto-tuning, ensuring the robot is stable enough to collect meaningful performance data.
PHASE 4 — Quantum Computing Based PID Auto-Tuning
4.1 Optimisation Problem Formulation
Frame PID gain tuning as an optimisation problem. Define a cost/fitness function based on system
performance metrics:
• Settling time
• Overshoot percentage
• Steady-state error
• ITAE — Integral of Time-weighted Absolute Error
The goal is to find the set of (Kp, Ki, Kd) values for both loops (6 parameters total) that minimises this cost
function.
4.2 Quantum Approach Options
• QAOA (Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm) — encode the optimisation landscape and use
quantum circuits to find optimal gains. Run on IBM Quantum, AWS Braket, or Qiskit Aer simulator.
• Quantum Annealing (D-Wave) — map the gain optimisation directly to a QUBO problem and run on
D-Wave Leap cloud. Best suited for this type of continuous optimisation.
• VQE (Variational Quantum Eigensolver) — viable as a hybrid classical-quantum approach, though less
common in control applications.
4.3 Hybrid Classical-Quantum Workflow
Since real quantum hardware has noise limitations, use a hybrid approach: the classical computer simulates
the robot and evaluates the cost function, while the quantum processor proposes candidate gain values.
Results are fed back iteratively until convergence.
4.4 Deployment
Once optimal gains are found offline via quantum tuning, flash them onto the embedded controller. Optionally
retune periodically when the digital twin detects performance degradation in the physical robot.
PHASE 5 — Digital Twin Development
5.1 Physics Simulation Model

title: Page 3
text:
Build a high-fidelity simulation using one of the following platforms:
• Webots — free, ROS-compatible, excellent for wheeled robots.
• Gazebo + ROS2 — industry standard, highly extensible.
• MATLAB/Simulink — best for control system design and formal analysis.
• Unity + Physics Engine — if an interactive or visual twin is required.
Model the full dynamics: inverted pendulum equations, ball rolling dynamics on a tilting platform, motor
dynamics, and sensor noise models.
5.2 Real-Time Data Synchronisation
Stream live sensor data from the physical robot to the digital twin over Wi-Fi/MQTT or ROS2 topics. The twin
mirrors the robot's state continuously in real time.
5.3 Twin Use Cases
• Test new PID gains in simulation before deploying to hardware (safe, zero-risk testing).
• Anomaly detection — if real robot behaviour diverges from the twin, flag a fault condition.
• Run the quantum optimiser against the simulated twin rather than the physical robot (faster, safer, fully
repeatable).
• Predict long-term wear and failure in motors and sensors over time.
5.4 Visualisation Dashboard
Build a live dashboard using ROS2 RViz, Grafana, or a custom web application. Display real-time tilt angle,
ball position, PID outputs, and a side-by-side twin vs. real comparison plot.
PHASE 6 — Integration & Testing
6.1 Unit Testing
Test each subsystem independently — IMU readings accuracy, ball detection reliability, motor response
latency, and individual PID loop stability before integration.
6.2 Integration Testing
Combine inner and outer loops progressively. First test robot balancing alone without the ball, then with the
ball placed statically, and finally with the ball given deliberate push disturbances.
6.3 Quantum Tuning Validation
Compare the performance of classically tuned gains versus quantum-tuned gains using defined metrics
(settling time, overshoot, ITAE). This comparative result is a key deliverable for the final year report.
6.4 Digital Twin Validation
Compare digital twin predicted behaviour against real robot behaviour across multiple test scenarios.
Quantify twin fidelity using RMSE or similar error metrics.
PHASE 7 — Documentation & Presentation
Write up the complete system architecture, mathematical models (state space equations, PID derivation,
quantum QUBO formulation), experimental results, and comparative analysis. Prepare a live demonstration
with the robot balancing the ball while the digital twin mirrors it on screen in real time.

title: Page 4
text:
Suggestions for Improvement
1. Add LQR as a Benchmark Controller
Linear Quadratic Regulator (LQR) is a modern optimal control method well-suited for this multi-state system.
Implement it alongside PID and compare results — it significantly strengthens the project academically and
often outperforms cascade PID on coupled systems like this.
2. Use an Extended Kalman Filter for State Estimation
Rather than a simple complementary filter, implement a full Extended Kalman Filter (EKF) to fuse all sensor
data into a clean state estimate. This improves both robot balancing and ball tracking accuracy and is a
strong addition to the report.
3. Reinforce the Quantum Justification
Quantum advantage for 6-parameter optimisation is difficult to prove over classical methods. Explicitly
compare quantum tuning against a classical metaheuristic such as PSO or Genetic Algorithm on the same
cost function, and discuss where quantum methods scale better — higher-dimensional search spaces, more
complex coupled systems.
4. Add Formal Disturbance Rejection Testing
Formally test and document how the system responds to external disturbances — pushing the robot, placing
the ball off-centre suddenly, or operating on uneven surfaces. This demonstrates robustness and makes for
compelling live demo content.
5. Use ROS2 as Middleware
ROS2 ties your hardware, digital twin, and dashboard together cleanly. It handles real-time communication,
logging, replay, and is the industry standard — a great addition to the project for both report quality and
career value.
6. Model Uncertainty in the Digital Twin
Add sensor noise, actuator lag, and model parameter uncertainty into the twin to make it a more realistic
simulation. This makes quantum-tuned gains more robust when finally deployed to real hardware.
7. Explore Gain Scheduling (Adaptive PID)
Consider scheduling PID gains based on operating conditions — for example, different gains when the ball is
near the centre versus near the edge of the platform. This is a practical and academically interesting
extension beyond fixed quantum-tuned gains.
Generated for Final Year Engineering Project | Self-Balancing Robot Pipeline Document
